title: BaxBench Can LLMs Generate Correct and Secure Backends
abstract: Automatic program generation has long been a fundamental challenge in computer science. Recent benchmarks have shown that large language models (LLMs) can effectively generate code at the function level, make code edits, and solve algorithmic coding tasks. However, to achieve full automation, LLMs should be able to generate production-quality, self-contained application modules. To evaluate the capabilities of LLMs in solving this challenge, we introduce BAXBENCH, a novel evaluation benchmark consisting of 392 tasks for the generation of backend applications. We focus on backends for three critical reasons: (i) they are practically relevant, building the core components of most modern web and cloud software, (ii) they are difficult to get right, requiring multiple functions and files to achieve the desired functionality, and (iii) they are security-critical, as they are exposed to untrusted third-parties, making secure solutions that prevent deployment-time attacks an imperative. BAXBENCH validates the functionality of the generated applications with comprehensive test cases, and assesses their security exposure by executing end-to-end exploits. Our experiments reveal key limitations of current LLMs in both functionality and security: (i) even the best model, OpenAI o1, achieves a mere 62% on code correctness; (ii) on average, we could successfully execute security exploits on around half of the correct programs generated by each LLM; and (iii) in less popular backend frameworks, models further struggle to generate correct and secure applications. Progress on BAXBENCH signifies important steps towards autonomous and secure software development with LLMs.
impact: This paper introduces a novel benchmark for evaluating both the correctness and security of program code generated by large language models. By highlighting vulnerabilities and errors in generated code, this work contributes to safer and more reliable LLM-driven software development. While our benchmark can help improve the robustness of LLM-based code generation, it could also be used to refine malicious strategies if applied unethically. However, we believe that the positive impact of our work far outweighs the potential negative impacts through malicious usage.

query_input.scenario: Deployment of LLM-generated backend code in production web services
query_input.issue: Code vulnerabilities such as SQL injection or command injection enable data theft or server compromise
hazard: LLM-based backend code generation produces server-side code with exploitable security vulnerabilities (e.g., injection flaws, broken auth) enabling remote attacks.
exposure: Developers and organizations deploying LLM-generated backend applications without thorough security auditing

hipporag_result.vulnerability: Insufficient security auditing practices for AI-generated code enable exploitable vulnerabilities to reach production.
hipporag_result.impact: Widespread data theft and server compromise across production web services via remote injection attacks.
hipporag_result.dose_response: LLM-based backend code generation producing exploitable security vulnerabilities, through deployment without thorough security auditing, triggers insufficient auditing practices allowing vulnerabilities in production, leading to widespread data theft and server compromise via remote injection attacks.

dose_response: LLM code generation produces exploitable vulnerabilities, which when deployed without auditing exposes systems to remote attacks, leading to data breaches and server compromise.
vulnerability: Developers' trust in LLM-generated code and absence of security auditing prior to production deployment.
Organizational norms that prioritize speed over security review for automated code generation.
impact: Data breaches exposing customer PII and financial records.
Server compromise enabling further lateral movement and service disruption.
